_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Dictionaries practice 1
questions.explanation: Updating `pen` does not create a second key. Adding `book` makes two keys total, and the current value stored under `pen` is 3.

questions.title: Dictionaries practice 2
questions.prompt: Which loop prints both each course name and its grade?
choices: for course, grade in grades.items():
for course, grade in grades:
for grade in grades.values, course:
for item in grades.keys().values():
questions.explanation: `items()` produces key-value pairs that can be unpacked into two variables. Iterating over the dictionary alone produces only keys.

questions.title: Trace Dictionaries
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Keys appear before colons and values appear after them. Square brackets use a key to find its value. The resulting output is "Ada\n{'name': 'Ada', 'credits': 18, 'major': 'CS'}".

questions.title: Dictionaries good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Dictionaries?
choices: Choose stable, descriptive keys.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Choose stable, descriptive keys. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Dictionaries practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
scores = {"quiz": 8}
print(scores.get("exam", 0))

scores = {"quiz": 8}
print(scores["exam"])
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: Bracket lookup is useful when a key is required. `get` is better when absence is normal and you have a sensible fallback.

questions.title: Dictionaries practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Membership checks keys'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
`"Ada" in student` looks through the keys, not the values. Use `"name" in student` to ask whether that field exists. Use `"Ada" in student.values()` only when you truly need to search values.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: `"Ada" in student` looks through the keys, not the values. Use `"name" in student` to ask whether that field exists. Use `"Ada" in student.values()` only when you truly need to search values.

questions.title: Dictionaries practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Dictionaries?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Use brackets when a missing key is an error.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use brackets when a missing key is an error. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Dictionaries practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Dictionaries?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Use `get` for an optional value with a fallback.
questions.explanation: Use `get` for an optional value with a fallback. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Dictionaries practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Dictionaries?
choices: Use `.items()` when a loop needs keys and values.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use `.items()` when a loop needs keys and values. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Dictionaries practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Dictionaries?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Remember that assigning the same key updates rather than duplicates it.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Remember that assigning the same key updates rather than duplicates it. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
